ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific term for stir-fried sliced potato; 'potato food product' (FOODON_03301710) is the closest defensible broad match. A more specific stir-fried preparation term is not available in the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Potato products and dishes) is the closest defensible match. A specific stir-fried sliced potato entry does not exist in FoodEx2 at this level of specificity.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for stir-fried sliced potato identified in current release.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical side dish serving of stir-fried sliced potato is approximately 120 g, equivalent to a small bowl or side plate portion as commonly served in a Singaporean hawker or home-cooked meal.

nutrient_source.title: Stir Fried Sliced Potato — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Stir Fried Sliced Potato (cr_id F14060015). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this entry at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 85 is based on the University of Sydney GI Database reference for boiled/cooked white potato (GI ~85, high category). Stir-frying with oil and vinegar may modestly lower the effective GI compared to boiled potato; however, in the absence of a direct stir-fried sliced potato GI measurement, the boiled white potato reference (GI 85) is used as a conservative analogous estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (85 × 18.6) / 100 ≈ 15.8, using 120 g serving size (15.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 1.2 = 18.6 g carbohydrate per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 259 mg/100 g with beneficial potassium (~420 mg/100 g) and dietary fibre (3.36 g/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 259 mg/100 g is in the borderline range for hypertension management; individuals following a DASH diet should limit portion size to 100–120 g, avoid adding extra salt during cooking, and rely on the naturally high potassium content of potato to partially offset sodium's pressor effect.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High GI (~85) partially offset by dietary fibre (3.36 g/100 g) and stir-fry preparation with vinegar
type2_diabetes.guidance: Potato has a high GI (~85); individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portions to 100–120 g per meal, pair with low-GI vegetables or protein to blunt postprandial glucose rise, and avoid combining with other high-GI staples in the same meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat (4.6 g/100 g), low saturated fat (~0.6 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, and high dietary fibre (3.36 g/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The low saturated fat content (~0.6 g/100 g), absence of cholesterol, and high dietary fibre (3.36 g/100 g) make this dish acceptable for cardiovascular disease management when prepared with minimal oil and no added salt; limit oil quantity during stir-frying to keep total fat below 5 g/100 g.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade vegetable (potato) containing solanine; dried chili also a nightshade ingredient
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Potato is a nightshade vegetable containing solanine, which may trigger or worsen joint inflammation in susceptible individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; those with confirmed nightshade sensitivity should substitute with sweet potato or taro and avoid dried chili in the preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Sliced Potato (炒土豆片) is a popular Singaporean and Chinese home-cooked vegetable side dish made by stir-frying thinly sliced raw potato in a wok with hot oil, aromatics such as garlic and dried chili, and optional vinegar to achieve a slightly tangy, crisp-tender texture. The dish is seasoned with salt and soy sauce. Unlike Western potato preparations (boiled, mashed, or deep-fried), this stir-fry method preserves a firm bite and a mildly starchy flavour with a fragrant, savoury aroma from the wok. Per 100 g, it provides 106 kcal, 2.24 g protein, 4.6 g fat, 15.5 g carbohydrate, 3.36 g dietary fibre, and 259 mg sodium. The high dietary fibre content (3.36 g/100 g) supports digestive health and partially moderates the glycaemic response. However, potato has a high glycaemic index (~85), making portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is naturally vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free, making it suitable for a wide range of dietary preferences. Clinically, the borderline sodium level (259 mg/100 g) warrants attention for hypertension management, while the high potassium content (~420 mg/100 g) and fibre provide offsetting cardiovascular benefits. Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should note that potato is a nightshade vegetable and may exacerbate joint inflammation in sensitive individuals.
common_user_queries: How many calories in stir-fried sliced potato?
Is stir-fried potato good for high blood pressure?
How much sodium in stir-fried potato?
Can I eat stir-fried potato with rheumatoid arthritis?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F14060015) at confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using boiled white potato as the reference food, which is the closest approved GI source available. Multilingual names are standard translations for this well-known dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, water_g, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific stir-fried sliced potato preparation are not individually reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for F14060015. Potassium value (~420 mg/100 g) is an estimate based on USDA FoodData Central data for cooked potato (SR Legacy #11356) and is consistent with the hypertension guidance in the input record. Saturated fat estimated at 0.6 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central data for stir-fried potato with vegetable oil (SR Legacy reference); this is an adapted estimate as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry. Cholesterol is 0.0 g confirmed — this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients. GI value of 85 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using boiled white potato as the reference; stir-frying with vinegar may modestly lower the effective GI in practice. The input record's GI source 'Embedded GI Database' is not an approved source under Project Veritas v2; the GI has been re-anchored to the University of Sydney GI Database. Rheumatoid arthritis has been retained as an additional health condition given the clinical relevance of nightshade sensitivity.
